surface.name: Get Started with Qovery, Test & Learn Locally, then Deploy Your First Application from Git
surface.selection_basis: The current Get Started page tells a new user to choose a use case and presents Test & Learn Locally as the path for first-time users exploring Qovery. It is the only path marked free, no cloud account or credit card needed, and about 15 minutes. Its Docker-based local-cluster guide ends by linking Deploy First App, whose current quickstart explicitly defines the first application journey and terminal live URL.

alternatives_considered.name: Deploy with AI Agent
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The page presents it as a peer AI-powered path using an external coding agent. The platform's general first-application tutorial remains a direct Console path and exposes the individual onboarding steps needed for this research.

alternatives_considered.name: Managed production cluster
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This path requires a customer cloud account and about 30 minutes of cluster provisioning before the same application flow. The docs explicitly position the local path for first-time learning without cloud costs.

alternatives_considered.name: Bring Your Own Kubernetes
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This route assumes an existing Kubernetes cluster and administrative access, so it is not the lowest-prerequisite documented route for a new evaluator.

alternatives_considered.name: Pre-built container image
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The first-application guide presents this as a peer source but requires an already built image and a connected registry. The Git route is labeled best for building an application from source and is the more directly documented developer path.

alternatives_considered.name: Helm chart
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The first-application guide directs Helm users to separate configuration documentation and does not use it for the main first-app walkthrough.

entry_point.developer_intent: Evaluate Qovery locally and deploy a first containerized web application to an observable live URL.
assumed_prior_state: The developer can authenticate with an identity provider supported by the current signup flow and accept Qovery's Terms.
The developer uses Linux, macOS, or Windows through WSL and can run a Docker daemon locally.
The machine has at least 4 CPU cores and 8 GB RAM available for Docker, plus Git, jq, curl, sed, and grep.
For the selected Git deployment branch, the developer owns or can access a GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket repository containing a working containerized web application and Dockerfile.
The application listens on all interfaces at a known port and can run within the local cluster's available resources.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: Once running, click the application URL to view your live app
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: Qovery has cloned the selected repository, built and deployed its container to the local k3s cluster, reports the application as Running, and exposes a generated URL that serves the live application.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The deployment status progresses from Building to Deploying to Running, then the generated `https://your-app-xxx.qovery.io` application URL opens and returns the application.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Once running, click the application URL to view your live app

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: Deploy Your First Application explicitly ends its Monitor Your Deployment sequence with Running status and access to the live app URL. Its database, Helm, custom domain, variables, auto-deploy, and registry topics are placed under Next Steps after this point.

prerequisites.requirement: A Qovery account. Current public documentation says first signup uses a Git provider, Google, or Microsoft and requires acceptance of the Terms, but does not reproduce the authenticated form and verification order.
A Qovery organization on the default Team-plan trial. The organization documentation says the first organization and project are created during signup.
A local Linux, macOS, or Windows WSL machine with at least 4 CPU cores and 8 GB RAM available for Docker.
A running Docker daemon, Git, jq, curl, sed, grep, and an operating-system-supported way to install the Qovery CLI.

A GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket repository containing the application source and a Dockerfile at its root or a known subdirectory. Qovery's general first-app guide does not select or link a canonical starter repository.
Qovery access to the selected Git repository, through the personal Git identity used at first sign-in or a configured organization Git token with the required provider permissions.
A known application port, with the process listening on `0.0.0.0` rather than localhost if the app is to be publicly reachable.

primary_path.action: Open Get Started with Qovery and select Test & Learn Locally.
primary_path.input: https://www.qovery.com/docs/getting-started/quickstart
primary_path.output: The Docker-based local evaluation path is selected.
primary_path.success_signal: The path promises a free local k3s cluster with full Qovery feature access, no cloud account, and no credit card.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A production, ephemeral, AI-agent, or existing-cluster goal should use one of the peer paths instead.

primary_path.action: Create a Qovery account, authenticate through a supported identity provider, and accept the Terms during registration.
primary_path.input: A supported Git provider, Google, or Microsoft identity according to the organization guide, plus the required Terms acceptance.
primary_path.output: A personal Qovery user account exists.
primary_path.success_signal: The developer can enter the Qovery Console and later authenticate the CLI.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The public docs do not reconstruct current screen order, provider consent, verification, or any organization-domain controls. The Terms and Git Repository Access guide require GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket for registration or first sign-in, while the organization guide additionally lists Google and Microsoft.

primary_path.action: Create the first organization and complete the initial project prompt presented during signup.
primary_path.input: The organization and project information requested by the authenticated flow.
primary_path.output: An organization exists on the default Team-plan trial, with an initial project according to the organization guide.
primary_path.success_signal: The organization is available as a CLI context and can own a cluster, project, environments, and services.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The public organization guide does not enumerate first-signup fields. Its statement that signup creates the first project conflicts with the separate first-application guide's instruction to create a project after prerequisites.

primary_path.action: Verify the supported local operating system, Docker resources, running Docker daemon, Git, and common utilities.
primary_path.input: Linux, macOS, or Windows WSL; at least 4 CPU cores and 8 GB RAM for Docker; Docker, Git, jq, curl, sed, and grep.
primary_path.output: The machine satisfies the documented local-cluster prerequisites.
primary_path.success_signal: Docker is running and the required commands are available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Insufficient Docker resources can leave workloads Pending later. Windows outside WSL is not listed for this local guide.

primary_path.action: Install the Qovery CLI using the documented operating-system method.
primary_path.input: For example, Homebrew on macOS, `curl -s https://get.qovery.com | bash` on Linux or macOS, Scoop on Windows, or a manually downloaded pinned release with checksum verification.
primary_path.output: The `qovery` command is installed.
primary_path.success_signal: The CLI can be invoked in the terminal.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The official page warns that the convenience install script does not verify binary integrity and recommends the manual pinned-release checksum route for security-sensitive environments.

primary_path.action: Run `qovery auth` and complete browser authentication.
primary_path.input: `qovery auth`; use `qovery auth --headless` when the terminal has no browser.
primary_path.output: The CLI is authenticated to the Qovery account.
primary_path.success_signal: Authentication completes and the CLI can access the user's organizations.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Browser launch or identity-provider authorization can require manual completion; headless mode is the documented alternative.

primary_path.action: Run `qovery context set` and select the organization.
primary_path.input: `qovery context set` and the organization chosen at the prompt.
primary_path.output: The CLI context points at the intended organization.
primary_path.success_signal: Subsequent Qovery commands target that organization.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The organization must already exist and be visible to the authenticated user.

primary_path.action: Run `qovery demo up` to provision the local demo cluster.
primary_path.input: `qovery demo up`.
primary_path.output: The CLI creates a local k3s cluster, installs the Qovery agent and required components, and configures networking and ingress.
primary_path.success_signal: The command completes successfully after its documented 5 to 10 minute installation.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: macOS and Windows users may be prompted for an admin password for host routing. Docker capacity, local networking, or component installation can block completion.

primary_path.action: Open the Qovery Console and verify the demo cluster.
primary_path.input: https://console.qovery.com
primary_path.output: The local demo cluster appears in the organization.
primary_path.success_signal: The cluster reports Connected and ready to use.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If the cluster is absent or not Connected, the application path cannot proceed; inspect the local installation and cluster state before continuing.

primary_path.action: Use the project created at signup or create the tutorial project in the Console.
primary_path.input: From the organization overview, New Project, a project name such as `My First Project`, then Create.
primary_path.output: A project exists to organize the first environment and application.
primary_path.success_signal: The project opens in the Console.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The two official pages do not resolve whether the signup-created project should be reused or the first-app guide's New Project action should create another. Reusing the existing project removes this action.

primary_path.action: Create an environment in the project and place it on the connected local cluster.
primary_path.input: Create Environment, a name such as the documented `production` example, an environment type, the local demo cluster, then Create.
primary_path.output: An empty Qovery environment exists on the local cluster.
primary_path.success_signal: The environment opens and can accept a new service.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The cluster must be Connected and available in the cluster selector.

primary_path.action: Start application creation from the environment and select Git Repository as the source.
primary_path.input: New Service, Create application, an application name such as `my-api`, and Application Source: Git Repository.
primary_path.output: The application creation flow requests repository settings.
primary_path.success_signal: Git provider, repository, branch, and root-path controls are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Container Registry and Helm are peer branches with different prerequisites.

primary_path.action: Authorize Qovery's access to the Git source if the selected repository is not already available.
primary_path.input: Personal Git provider access from first sign-in, or an organization Git token configured with the provider-specific repository and webhook permissions.
primary_path.output: Qovery can list and clone the repository and manage deployment webhooks.
primary_path.success_signal: The target repository is selectable in application creation.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The first-app guide describes first-time provider authorization, while the current Git-access guide recommends creating and saving an organization token. Token generation adds provider-specific permission and copy-once steps.

primary_path.action: Select the repository, branch, and Dockerfile root path.
primary_path.input: A GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket repository, a branch such as `main`, and blank Root Application Path when the Dockerfile is at repository root or the correct subfolder otherwise.
primary_path.output: Qovery has a build source and Dockerfile location.
primary_path.success_signal: The selected branch contains the Dockerfile at the configured path.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The general guide provides examples but no canonical starter repository. A missing or invalid Dockerfile causes the build to fail.

primary_path.action: Keep or adjust the initial compute allocation.
primary_path.input: Default 500m vCPU, 512 MB RAM, and one instance unless the application requires different resources.
primary_path.output: The application has a resource request compatible with the local cluster.
primary_path.success_signal: The default or adjusted vCPU, RAM, and instance values are set for the application.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: An application or local cluster without enough CPU or memory can remain Pending. Reduce service resources or increase local Docker capacity.

primary_path.action: Define the application's public port and protocol.
primary_path.input: The port actually used by the application, a supported protocol, Publicly Accessible enabled, and external port 443 for HTTPS.
primary_path.output: Qovery can create ingress and a generated public application URL.
primary_path.success_signal: The port configuration matches the container process listening on `0.0.0.0`.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A port mismatch or localhost-only binding causes failed health checks or an unreachable application. Correct the app binding or Qovery port and redeploy.

primary_path.action: Optionally configure health checks and environment variables, then proceed without them when the selected application needs neither.
primary_path.input: Optional liveness or readiness probes, API keys, configuration values, feature flags, or secrets.
primary_path.output: The minimal application configuration is ready for review.
primary_path.success_signal: Required application-specific variables are present and optional controls can be deferred.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The guide says both areas are optional, but a developer-supplied repository may make variables or a specific health endpoint operationally required.

primary_path.action: Click Create and Deploy.
primary_path.input: The reviewed Git source, resource, port, health-check, and variable configuration.
primary_path.output: Qovery creates the application and starts its build and deployment.
primary_path.success_signal: Build logs, deployment logs, and application logs become available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Choosing Create instead stops before first success and requires a later explicit deploy action.

primary_path.action: Clone the repository, build and push the container image, create Kubernetes resources and ingress, configure HTTPS, and start the application.
primary_path.input: The selected Git commit and application configuration.
primary_path.output: A deployed application on the local k3s cluster.
primary_path.success_signal: The status progresses from Building to Deploying to Running, typically in the documented 2 to 5 minutes after deployment begins.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Build errors, image creation errors, Pending pods, crashes, or failed health checks keep the application from reaching Running. Use the corresponding logs and official troubleshooting actions before redeploying.

primary_path.action: Click the generated application URL and verify the live response.
primary_path.input: The generated URL in the form `https://your-app-xxx.qovery.io`.
primary_path.output: The browser requests the first deployed application through Qovery-managed ingress and HTTPS.
primary_path.success_signal: The expected application loads at the generated URL while the service remains Running.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If the URL does not respond, confirm Publicly Accessible, port and `0.0.0.0` binding, health checks, DNS propagation, and application logs. The guide allows 2 to 3 minutes for DNS propagation.

candidate_paths.name: Deploy a Git-backed application through the Console to a local k3s cluster
candidate_paths.selection_basis: Qovery's Get Started page explicitly recommends the free local route for first-time users and links the current Deploy Your First Application tutorial. Within that tutorial, Git Repository is the source path described as best for building from source.
candidate_paths.relationship_to_primary_path: This is the lowest-cost current vendor-presented evaluation path, but it still requires a developer-supplied Dockerfile repository because the general tutorial does not name a canonical sample.

branches.path: The organization guide lists GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Google, or Microsoft; the Terms require a supported Git provider for registration, and the Git Repository Access guide says first Console sign-in uses GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Provider screens and repository-access state differ, and Google or Microsoft login may still require a separate Git integration for a Git-backed app.

branches.path: Use Homebrew, convenience script, Scoop, Docker, or manual pinned-release installation with checksum verification.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Native installations converge on the `qovery` command, but installation effort and integrity verification differ. The Docker tab only demonstrates a containerized `help` command and does not reconstruct persistent authentication, context, or `demo up` invocation for this route.

branches.condition: Signup already created a usable project
branches.path: Reuse and open the signup-created project rather than creating another project.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Removes one project-creation action and avoids duplicate project state.

branches.path: Use personal Git access from first sign-in, or create a provider token and save it under Organization Settings, Git Repository Access.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The recommended token branch adds provider token permissions, token creation, copy, validation, and save actions before repository selection.

branches.path: Use a connected container registry and a specific pre-built image tag instead of a Git repository, or follow the separate Helm guide.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Skips Qovery's source build but adds registry or chart prerequisites and rejoins at resource and port configuration.

branches.condition: Application requires runtime configuration or health endpoints
branches.path: Add the required variables or secrets and configure liveness or readiness probes before Create and Deploy.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds application-specific fields that the general guide cannot enumerate.

friction_gates.description: The public docs establish identity providers and Terms acceptance but do not expose the atomic signup and verification sequence.
The signup guide says a first project is created during signup, while the first-app guide tells the same user to create a project after prerequisites.
The free route needs substantial local Docker capacity and several installed utilities.
Qovery warns that its convenience CLI installation script does not verify the binary's integrity; a pinned manual download with checksum verification is the documented safer alternative.
Local k3s and Qovery component installation takes about 5 to 10 minutes and can prompt for administrator access on macOS and Windows.
Repository access can require broad provider permissions or a separately created organization token before the repository is selectable.
The general first-app guide does not provide a canonical starter repository, so code, Dockerfile, dependencies, branch, and root path depend on the developer's own artifact.
The user must know the actual application port and all-interface binding; a mismatch prevents successful routing or health checks.
Use build, deployment, and application logs to distinguish Dockerfile failures, resource exhaustion, crashes, and health-check failures, correct the cause, and redeploy.
A Running deployment can still require 2 to 3 minutes of DNS propagation before its generated URL is reachable.

time_to_first_success.value: The Get Started page estimates about 15 minutes for Test & Learn Locally. The local cluster guide assigns about 5 to 10 minutes to `qovery demo up`, and the first-application guide says a deployment typically takes 2 to 5 minutes. These claims do not separately budget account creation, repository preparation, Git authorization, or application-specific fixes.

sources.title: Deploy Your First Application
evidence_supported: Resource, port, health and variable settings
Running status and live URL boundary

uncertainties.question: What exact signup screens, verification steps, Terms checkbox, organization fields, and project fields does a new user encounter today?
uncertainties.impact: The account segment cannot be atomically counted and may include provider consent or organizational controls not visible in public docs.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Current official public docs summarize identity and creation outcomes but do not reproduce the authenticated onboarding screens.

uncertainties.question: Should a first-time developer reuse the project created at signup or create the additional project named by the first-app guide?
uncertainties.impact: The path differs by one project creation flow and can create duplicate project state.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Two current official pages describe incompatible assumptions and provide no reconciliation.

uncertainties.question: Which canonical repository and Dockerfile should a completely new evaluator use?
uncertainties.impact: Repository creation, application code, Dockerfile authoring, dependency installation, port selection, and variables can add a large undocumented segment before Qovery onboarding.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The current general first-app guide provides example names and settings but no starter repository or complete sample artifact.

uncertainties.question: Does the local k3s route expose the exact public `qovery.io` URL documented in the generic first-app guide on every supported operating system?
uncertainties.impact: The final verification mechanism and possible local routing or privilege steps may differ from the generic guide.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The local-cluster guide says networking and ingress are configured and applications can be deployed locally, but it does not show the first application's exact URL or response example.

uncertainties.question: What are the current free-trial duration, expiration behavior, and resource quotas?
uncertainties.impact: Workshop analysis cannot quantify trial pressure or account survival before first success.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Current official pricing and Terms pages name a free trial but do not publish these details, while the local path is separately described as free and no-card.

excluded_after_success: Adding a managed database or lifecycle job.
Deploying Helm charts or configuring additional container registries.
Configuring automatic deployments or preview environments for pull requests.
Production cloud cluster provisioning, autoscaling, backups, monitoring integrations, and hardening.
Organization-wide Git token rotation and long-term repository governance.
Destroying the local demo cluster with `qovery demo destroy`.
